subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Open your eyes right now and look around you. Perhaps you can see the sunlight coming through a window, the pages of this book, your own hand, or the face of someone you love. Have you ever stopped to wonder how you are able to see any of it? In this unit you will discover the answer, and it is one of the most wonderful stories in all of science. The answer is light.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 5 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Identify light sources and tell the difference between objects that make their own light and objects that only reflect light.
Explain that light travels in straight lines, and use this idea to explain shadows and why you cannot see around corners.
Describe how you see an object: light travels from a source, bounces off the object, and enters your eye.
Investigate how mirrors reflect light, and use two mirrors to see hidden things.
Name the main parts of the eye and explain, in simple words, how it lets you see.
Explain why still water at dawn shows a clear reflection but the same water shows no reflection when the wind makes waves.
Name two things Abdi did to protect his eyes from the strong sun, and explain why eye protection matters.
Explain that light is a form of energy that travels in straight lines.

concepts.title: Light Sources and How Light Travels
concepts.explanation: Objects that make light, and objects that only reflect it Let us begin with a simple question that hides a deep idea. Look around your home and think about everything you can see. Which of those things actually make their own light? The answer is: only a few. The sun blazing over the Rift Valley makes its own light. A candle flame makes its own light. A torch, a cooking fire, a lightning flash, and a glowing phone screen all make their own light. We call all of these light sources.
concepts.example: Now think about everything else: this book, your clothes, a cup, a goat in the yard, a wall, the moon in the night sky. None of these make their own light. You can only see them because light from a light source lands…

concepts.title: How We See Things
concepts.explanation: Now we can put the pieces together and answer the big question: how do you actually see? Many children (and even many grown-ups) secretly believe that seeing works like a beam shooting out of your eyes onto an object, the way a torch shines. This is completely wrong, and proving it wrong is one of the joys of this unit. Your eyes do not send out any light at all. Your eyes are catchers, not throwers. Here is what really happens, step by step, when Khadija looks at a ripe red mango on the table:
concepts.example: Light leaves a light source, for example the sun through the window or the lamp above the table.

concepts.title: Reflection and Mirrors
concepts.explanation: You already know that light bounces, or reflects, off objects. Most surfaces are slightly rough, so they scatter the light in every direction. That is why you can see this page from any angle but you cannot see your face in it. A mirror is different. A mirror is extremely smooth and shiny, so it reflects nearly all the light in a neat, orderly way. The rays stay organised, so instead of a blur you see a clear picture, an image. Nature makes mirrors too. On a calm, windless morning, the surface of a pond, a water…
concepts.example: There is a neat rule for how light bounces off a mirror. The light comes in at some angle and leaves at the same angle on the other side, like a football bouncing off a smooth wall. Scientists say 'the angle of…

concepts.title: Transparent, Translucent, and Opaque Materials
concepts.explanation: Not everything treats light the same way. Some things let light pour straight through, some let a little through, and some stop it completely. Scientists sort materials into three groups based on how they handle light. A transparent material lets almost all the light pass straight through, so you can see clearly through it. Clear window glass is transparent, and so is clean water in a glass and clear plastic wrap. That is why houses have glass windows: they let the daylight flood in while keeping out the wind,…
concepts.example: A translucent material lets some light through but scatters it, so the light that comes out is soft and blurry and you cannot see clearly through it. Thin cloth, wax paper, frosted bathroom glass, and a thin plastic…

concepts.title: Bending Light (Refraction)
concepts.explanation: You have learned that light travels in straight lines. That is true, but there is one important situation where light changes direction: when it passes from one clear material into another, such as from air into water. This bending of light is called refraction, and it leads to some surprising sights. Try this in your mind, and later with a real glass. Put a straight straw or a pencil into a clear glass of water and look from the side. The straw appears to bend or break right at the surface of the water. Lift it…
concepts.example: Refraction happens all around the coast and the rivers. If Sagal tries to grab a fish she sees in the shallow water of the Jubba River, the fish is not exactly where it appears to be; refraction has shifted its apparent…

explorations.title: Light Source or Reflector?
explorations.context: To find out which objects in your home make their own light and which only reflect light from another source.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, predict: which of these do you think make their own light: a torch, a mirror, a phone screen, a white shirt, the moon? Write your guesses.
explorations.answer: Which objects could you see in total darkness? What does that tell you about them? Why did the mirror and paper appear only when the torch was on? The moon is bright at night but it is in the reflector column. Explain why.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out which objects in your home make their own light and which only reflect light from another source.

explorations.title: Does Light Travel in Straight Lines?
explorations.context: To prove that light travels in straight lines and cannot bend around corners on its own.
explorations.prompt: Predict: if you shine a torch through a straight tube, will light come out the far end? What if the tube is bent? Write your predictions and your reasons.
explorations.answer: Why did light fail to come out of the bent tube? Why did moving one hole out of line block the light? How does this experiment explain why shadows form and why you cannot see round corners?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To prove that light travels in straight lines and cannot bend around corners on its own.

explorations.title: The Bending Straw (Refraction)
explorations.context: To observe refraction, the bending of light, and see why a straw looks broken in water.
explorations.prompt: Predict what a straight straw will look like when you put it into a glass of water and look from the side. Draw your prediction.
explorations.answer: Did the straw really bend? If not, what actually bent? Why did the coin seem to move when water was added? How does this connect to a fisherman missing a fish he sees in shallow water?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To observe refraction, the bending of light, and see why a straw looks broken in water.

explorations.title: Mirror Magic and a Simple Periscope
explorations.context: To explore how mirrors reflect light and to build a two-mirror periscope that lets you see over or around something.
explorations.prompt: Predict: can you use two mirrors to see something that is not directly in front of you? How do you think the light will travel?
explorations.answer: Why do you need exactly two mirrors, not one, to see over the top? What happens to the view if you tilt one mirror at the wrong angle? How is your periscope like the one a submarine uses beneath the Indian Ocean?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To explore how mirrors reflect light and to build a two-mirror periscope that lets you see over or around something.

explorations.title: Transparent, Translucent, or Opaque?
explorations.context: To classify household materials by how much light they let through, and to link each type to the shadow it makes.
explorations.prompt: Predict which of these will let light through: clear plastic wrap, wax or greaseproof paper, aluminium foil, thin cloth, cardboard. Write your guesses.
explorations.answer: How does the shadow help you decide which group a material belongs to? Why is window glass transparent but frosted bathroom glass translucent, and why is each useful? Which material would be best to soften a harsh lamp, and why?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To classify household materials by how much light they let through, and to link each type to the shadow it makes.

methods.title: Light Source or Reflector?
methods.example: To find out which objects in your home make their own light and which only reflect light from another source.
steps: In a normal lit room, list ten objects you can see.
Now make the room as dark as you can. Which objects can you still see glowing on their own? Write them down.
Turn on only the torch. Notice that the mirror, the paper, and other objects suddenly appear again because they reflect the torchlight.
Cover the torch completely with your hand. Notice everything vanishes again.
Sort your ten objects into two columns: makes its own light and only reflects light.

methods.title: Does Light Travel in Straight Lines?
methods.example: To prove that light travels in straight lines and cannot bend around corners on its own.
steps: Shine the torch through the straight tube and look at the far end. Record what you see.
Gently bend the tube into a curve and shine the torch again. Record what happens.
Make a small hole in the centre of each of the three cardboard pieces.
Stand them up in a row, about a hand-width apart, so the three holes line up straight. Shine the torch through the first hole and see if light reaches the far side.
Slide the middle card sideways so its hole is no longer in line. Shine again and record.

methods.title: The Bending Straw (Refraction)
methods.example: To observe refraction, the bending of light, and see why a straw looks broken in water.
steps: Fill the clear glass about three-quarters full with clean water.
Put the straw into the water and look at the glass from the side, at the level of the water surface.
Notice how the straw appears to bend or break at the water line. Draw exactly what you see.
Slowly lift the straw out and see that it was straight all along.
Extra test: place a coin at the bottom of the empty glass, step back until it just disappears behind the rim, then have someone pour in water. Watch the coin seem to rise into view.

methods.title: Mirror Magic and a Simple Periscope
methods.example: To explore how mirrors reflect light and to build a two-mirror periscope that lets you see over or around something.
steps: First, explore one mirror: shine the torch at it from different angles and watch where the reflected spot lands on the wall. Notice the light bounces off at the same angle it arrived.
Cut a viewing hole near the top of one side of the box and another near the bottom of the opposite side.
Tape one mirror at the top, tilted at a slant, facing the top hole.
Tape the second mirror at the bottom, tilted the opposite way, facing the bottom hole.
Look into the bottom hole. You should be able to see over the top, as if you were taller. Aim it round a doorway or over a wall.

methods.title: Transparent, Translucent, or Opaque?
methods.example: To classify household materials by how much light they let through, and to link each type to the shadow it makes.
steps: Darken the room a little so shadows show clearly.
Hold the first material between the torch and the wall.
Observe how much light passes through and what kind of shadow appears on the wall.
Decide: transparent (clear shadow-free), translucent (fuzzy grey shadow), or opaque (dark sharp shadow).
Repeat for every material and fill in your table.

workedExamples.title: Tracing a Light Path
workedExamples.prompt: Some science needs careful step-by-step thinking, just like a maths problem. Here is a worked example of tracing the path of light so you can see an object. Follow every step.
workedExamples.solution: Problem: Halima is sitting in the evening. There is a lamp on the shelf. On the table there is a yellow banana. Halima can see the banana clearly. Draw and describe the complete path of the light, step by step. Step 1 - Find the light source. Nothing can be seen without a source. The banana does not glow, and Halima's eyes do not shine. The only source here is the lamp. So the light story must begin at the lamp. Step 2 - Light travels in straight lines to the object. Light leaves the lamp and shoots out in…

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: Light travels in: (a) curved lines (b) straight lines (c) zigzags (d) circles
workedExamples.solution: 2 b) straight lines This is a basic property of light.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: We can see the moon at night because it: (a) makes its own light (b) reflects sunlight (c) glows in the dark (d) is on fire
workedExamples.solution: 3 b) reflects sunlight The moon is a reflector, not a light source.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: We see a book because light: (a) goes from our eyes to the book (b) is made by the book (c) reflects off the book into our eyes (d) bends around the book
workedExamples.solution: 4 c) reflects off the book into our eyes Eyes catch light; they do not send it.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: A material that lets no light through is: (a) transparent (b) translucent (c) opaque (d) reflective
workedExamples.solution: 5 c) opaque Opaque means no light passes through.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Frosted bathroom glass is best described as: (a) transparent (b) translucent (c) opaque (d) a light source
workedExamples.solution: 6 b) translucent Frosted glass scatters light so you cannot see clearly.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: A straw looks bent in water because light: (a) reflects (b) refracts (bends) (c) stops (d) speeds up forever
workedExamples.solution: 7 b) refracts (bends) Light bends leaving the water, so the straw looks bent.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: To see around a corner you usually need: (a) one flat mirror at any angle (b) two mirrors at the right angles (c) a torch only (d) no mirror
workedExamples.solution: 8 b) two mirrors at the right angles One mirror is not enough to turn a corner.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a light source? (a) a mirror (b) the moon (c) a candle flame (d) a white wall
practice.answer: 1 c) a candle flame A candle makes its own light; the others only reflect.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Light travels in: (a) curved lines (b) straight lines (c) zigzags (d) circles
practice.answer: 2 b) straight lines This is a basic property of light.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: We can see the moon at night because it: (a) makes its own light (b) reflects sunlight (c) glows in the dark (d) is on fire
practice.answer: 3 b) reflects sunlight The moon is a reflector, not a light source.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: We see a book because light: (a) goes from our eyes to the book (b) is made by the book (c) reflects off the book into our eyes (d) bends around the book
practice.answer: 4 c) reflects off the book into our eyes Eyes catch light; they do not send it.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A material that lets no light through is: (a) transparent (b) translucent (c) opaque (d) reflective
practice.answer: 5 c) opaque Opaque means no light passes through.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Frosted bathroom glass is best described as: (a) transparent (b) translucent (c) opaque (d) a light source
practice.answer: 6 b) translucent Frosted glass scatters light so you cannot see clearly.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A straw looks bent in water because light: (a) reflects (b) refracts (bends) (c) stops (d) speeds up forever
practice.answer: 7 b) refracts (bends) Light bends leaving the water, so the straw looks bent.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: To see around a corner you usually need: (a) one flat mirror at any angle (b) two mirrors at the right angles (c) a torch only (d) no mirror
practice.answer: 8 b) two mirrors at the right angles One mirror is not enough to turn a corner.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The part of the eye that changes size to control how much light enters is the: (a) retina (b) pupil (c) optic nerve (d) cornea
practice.answer: 9 b) pupil The pupil widens in the dark and shrinks in bright light.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: An opaque object in sunlight makes a: (a) faint fuzzy shadow (b) no shadow (c) dark sharp shadow (d) coloured shadow
practice.answer: 10 c) dark sharp shadow Opaque objects block all the light.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Still water shows a clear reflection but choppy water does not because choppy water: (a) is not transparent (b) makes its own light (c) scatters the reflected light (d) absorbs all light
practice.answer: 11 c) scatters the reflected light A rough surface sends light in all directions.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A red shirt looks red because it: (a) makes red light (b) reflects red light and absorbs the rest (c) absorbs red light (d) bends red light
practice.answer: 12 b) reflects red light and absorbs the rest Colour seen is the colour reflected.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Light Source or Reflector?
activities.materials: A torch; A mirror; A phone or screen (with permission); A sheet of white paper; A dark room, such as a store-room at night, or a cupboard
steps: In a normal lit room, list ten objects you can see.
Now make the room as dark as you can. Which objects can you still see glowing on their own? Write them down.
Turn on only the torch. Notice that the mirror, the paper, and other objects suddenly appear again because they reflect the torchlight.
Cover the torch completely with your hand. Notice everything vanishes again.
Sort your ten objects into two columns: makes its own light and only reflects light.

activities.title: Does Light Travel in Straight Lines?
activities.materials: A torch; A cardboard tube (from kitchen roll), or a rolled sheet of cardboard; Three pieces of cardboard about the size of your hand; A pencil to make holes; A piece of string; A dark room
steps: Shine the torch through the straight tube and look at the far end. Record what you see.
Gently bend the tube into a curve and shine the torch again. Record what happens.
Make a small hole in the centre of each of the three cardboard pieces.
Stand them up in a row, about a hand-width apart, so the three holes line up straight. Shine the torch through the first hole and see if light reaches the far side.
Slide the middle card sideways so its hole is no longer in line. Shine again and record.

activities.title: The Bending Straw (Refraction)
activities.materials: A clear glass or jar; Clean water; A straight straw, a pencil, or a long spoon; A coin (for the extra test)
steps: Fill the clear glass about three-quarters full with clean water.
Put the straw into the water and look at the glass from the side, at the level of the water surface.
Notice how the straw appears to bend or break at the water line. Draw exactly what you see.
Slowly lift the straw out and see that it was straight all along.
Extra test: place a coin at the bottom of the empty glass, step back until it just disappears behind the rim, then have someone pour in water. Watch the coin seem to rise into view.

activities.title: Mirror Magic and a Simple Periscope
activities.materials: Two small mirrors (plastic if possible); A torch; A long cardboard box or a tall milk carton; Tape; Scissors (ask an adult to help cut)
steps: First, explore one mirror: shine the torch at it from different angles and watch where the reflected spot lands on the wall. Notice the light bounces off at the same angle it arrived.
Cut a viewing hole near the top of one side of the box and another near the bottom of the opposite side.
Tape one mirror at the top, tilted at a slant, facing the top hole.
Tape the second mirror at the bottom, tilted the opposite way, facing the bottom hole.
Look into the bottom hole. You should be able to see over the top, as if you were taller. Aim it round a doorway or over a wall.

activities.title: Transparent, Translucent, or Opaque?
activities.materials: A torch; A white wall or sheet of paper as a screen; Clear plastic wrap; Wax or greaseproof paper, or thin cloth; Aluminium foil or cardboard; Any other materials you are curious about
steps: Darken the room a little so shadows show clearly.
Hold the first material between the torch and the wall.
Observe how much light passes through and what kind of shadow appears on the wall.
Decide: transparent (clear shadow-free), translucent (fuzzy grey shadow), or opaque (dark sharp shadow).
Repeat for every material and fill in your table.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: the key words: source, reflect, transparent, translucent, opaque, refraction.

terms: Light
A form of energy that travels and lets us see.
Light source
An object that makes its own light (sun, candle, torch).
Reflect / Reflection
When light bounces off a surface; also the mirror image.
Ray
A straight line showing the path light travels.
Transparent
Lets almost all light through; you see clearly (glass, water).
Translucent
Lets some scattered light through; you see fuzzily (wax paper).
Opaque
Blocks all light (wood, metal, your body).
Shadow
A dark area where an opaque object blocks the light.
Mirror
A smooth shiny surface that reflects light to form a clear image.
Refraction
The bending of light passing from one clear material into another.
Vision
Seeing, done by the eyes and brain together.
Pupil
The opening that lets light into the eye; changes size with brightness.
commonMistakes: Eyes shoot out rays to see.
The moon makes its own light.
A straw really bends in water.

realProblems.prompt: Use what you have learned to solve these real-life problems.
realProblems.answer: She cannot see because there is no light to reflect off the sandals into her eyes. She could switch on a light or bring a torch, or open a door or window to let daylight in.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: She cannot see because there is no light to reflect off the sandals into her eyes. She could switch on a light or bring a torch, or open a door or window to let daylight in.

realProblems.prompt: Zahra is looking for her sandals in a dark store-room and cannot see them at all. Explain why, and give two ways she could see them.
realProblems.answer: A mirror reflects the light coming from cars behind and sends it into the driver's eyes, so the driver can see behind without turning around, thanks to reflection.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: A mirror reflects the light coming from cars behind and sends it into the driver's eyes, so the driver can see behind without turning around, thanks to reflection.

realProblems.prompt: A driver on the busy road near Eastleigh needs to see cars coming up behind. Explain how mirrors solve this problem using reflection.
realProblems.answer: Frosted (translucent) glass, because it lets daylight into the bathroom while scattering the light so that people outside cannot see clearly through it, giving privacy.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Frosted (translucent) glass, because it lets daylight into the bathroom while scattering the light so that people outside cannot see clearly through it, giving privacy.

realProblems.prompt: A family near Mombasa wants daylight in the bathroom but also privacy. What kind of glass should the window be, and why?
realProblems.answer: Refraction bends the light coming from the fish as it leaves the water, so the fish is not exactly where it appears. He should aim a little lower or to the side of where he sees it.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Refraction bends the light coming from the fish as it leaves the water, so the fish is not exactly where it appears. He should aim a little lower or to the side of where he sees it.

realProblems.prompt: Yusuf tries to scoop a fish he sees just under the water of the Jubba River but keeps missing. Explain what is tricking him and how he should adjust.
realProblems.answer: Light colours reflect most sunlight and heat away, keeping him cooler, while dark colours absorb it. A hat shades the eyes and face, and keeping the strong sun off the eyes protects the retina from damage.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Light colours reflect most sunlight and heat away, keeping him cooler, while dark colours absorb it. A hat shades the eyes and face, and keeping the strong sun off the eyes protects the retina from damage.

realProblems.prompt: During a hot, bright afternoon on the savanna, explain why wearing a light-coloured headscarf and a hat helps, using ideas about reflecting and absorbing light and protecting the eyes.
realProblems.answer: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: In your own words, what is the difference between a light source and a reflector?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A light source makes its own light (sun, candle, torch). A reflector only bounces back light from a source (moon, mirror, paper) and cannot be seen in total darkness.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain why light traveling in straight lines causes shadows to form.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Light cannot bend around an opaque object or pass through it, so the straight rays are blocked and leave a dark shadow shaped like the object behind it.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Describe the complete path of light that lets you see a green leaf in daylight.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Light from a source (the sun) travels in a straight line to the leaf, reflects off it as green light, travels into the eye, and the brain reads it as a green leaf.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why does the moon shine even though it has no fire?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The moon has no fire of its own; it reflects sunlight that hits it and bounces down to us, like a giant mirror in the sky.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between a translucent and an opaque material? Give one example of each.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A translucent material lets some scattered light through so you cannot see clearly (example: wax paper or thin cloth). An opaque material lets no light through (example: wood or metal).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain what refraction is and give one everyday example of it.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Refraction is the bending of light as it passes from one clear material into another, such as air into water. Example: a straw looking bent in a glass of water.

questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: scatters the reflected light A rough surface sends light in all directions.

selfAssessment: I can identify light sources and tell the difference between objects that make their own light and objects that only reflect light.
I can explain that light travels in straight lines, and use this idea to explain shadows and why you cannot see around corners.
I can describe how you see an object: light travels from a source, bounces off the object, and enters your eye.
I can investigate how mirrors reflect light, and use two mirrors to see hidden things.
I can name the main parts of the eye and explain, in simple words, how it lets you see.
I can explain why still water at dawn shows a clear reflection but the same water shows no reflection when the wind makes waves.
I can name two things Abdi did to protect his eyes from the strong sun, and explain why eye protection matters.
I can explain that light is a form of energy that travels in straight lines.
